sq_001.text: A figure detaches from the shadows of a recessed doorway as you pass. Tall, gaunt, wearing a coat that seems to absorb light. A respirator mask hides the lower half of their face, but the eyes above it are sharp and appraising. "You look like someone who knows how to find things," the figure says, voice filtered through the mask into a metallic rasp. "I collect... rarities. Data chips from the old world. Three of them are somewhere in this city, and I'll pay handsomely for their recovery."

choices.text: "What kind of data chips?"
"How handsomely are we talking?"
Walk away without engaging

sq_002.text: The Collector tilts their head. "Pre-Collapse military encryption chips. Each one contains fragments of something... significant. The first is in a derelict server farm in the Industrial Bottoms. The second was last seen in a pawnshop in Midtown. The third..." They pause. "The third is held by someone who knows its value. A data broker named Silk, operates out of the Neon Narrows."

choices.text: "I'll start with the server farm."
"The pawnshop sounds easier."
"Tell me about Silk."

sq_003.text: A dry laugh crackles through the respirator. "Five thousand credits per chip. Fifteen thousand if you bring all three. That's more than most people in this district see in a year." The Collector produces a small holographic card and holds it between two gloved fingers. "My contact frequency. When you have a chip, ping me. I'll arrange the exchange."

choices.text: Take the card and ask about the chips' locations
Take the card and head out to search on your own
"Not interested in running errands."

sq_004.text: The Industrial Bottoms. A graveyard of manufacturing ambition, where automated factories once churned out consumer goods for the towers above. Now the massive buildings stand dark and gutted, their walls streaked with chemical runoff and spray-tagged with gang signs. You find the server farm in a sub-basement beneath a collapsed plastics plant. The entrance is a rusted service hatch half-buried in debris. A faded warning sign reads: MERIDIAN CORP — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY — LETHAL COUNTERMEASURES ACTIVE.

choices.text: Try to hack the security panel beside the hatch
Force the hatch open manually
Search for another way in

sq_005.text: The pawnshop is called Lucky's Second Chances, wedged between a noodle stand and a body-mod clinic in Midtown's market strip. The owner, a heavyset man with cybernetic eyes that whir and click as they refocus, watches you from behind a cage of reinforced wire. Behind him, shelves groan under the weight of salvaged tech, old weapons, and personal effects from people who had to sell everything just to eat.

choices.text: Ask about pre-Collapse data chips
Browse the inventory first to seem casual
Try to intimidate him into handing it over

sq_006.text: The Collector's eyes narrow above the respirator. "Silk is... dangerous. A data broker who operates from a club called the Frequency in Neon Narrows. They deal in stolen information, blackmail material, corporate secrets. The chip came into Silk's possession through channels I'd rather not describe. Silk knows what it contains and won't part with it easily. You'll need leverage, charm, or violence. I leave the methodology to you."

choices.text: "I'll deal with Silk. Where's the Frequency?"
"I'll start with the easier chips first."
"This sounds like more trouble than it's worth."

sq_007.text: Your fingers dance across the corroded keypad, bypassing decades of encryption that was cutting-edge when it was installed. The security protocols unravel like old thread. The hatch hisses and slides open, releasing a gust of stale, recycled air from below. A ladder descends into darkness. Emergency lighting activates as you climb down — dim red strips that illuminate a corridor lined with dead server racks, their indicator lights dark for years.

choices.text: Proceed carefully down the corridor
Check the server racks for the chip as you go

sq_008.text: You brace yourself and wrench the hatch upward. Metal screams against metal, rust flaking away in orange clouds. The hinges give with a tortured groan, and the hatch flies open. But the noise echoes through the empty industrial landscape. Somewhere in the darkness below, something mechanical activates — a whirring, clicking sound that grows louder. An old security drone, still running on backup power after all these years.

choices.text: Drop down quickly before the drone arrives
Prepare to fight the drone

sq_009.text: You circle the collapsed plastics plant, scanning the structure for alternative entry points. Around the back, you find a ventilation shaft with a rusted-out grate. The shaft is narrow and dark, angling downward at a steep gradient. It's a tight squeeze, but it bypasses whatever security might be on the main entrance. The only question is whether the shaft holds your weight or drops you into something worse.

choices.text: Squeeze through the ventilation shaft
Go back and try the main hatch instead

sq_010.text: Lucky's cybernetic eyes zoom in on you with an audible click. "Pre-Collapse? Now that's a specific ask. Most people come in here looking for cheap chrome or weapons they can't legally buy." He reaches under the counter and produces a small case. Inside, nestled in foam, is a military-grade data chip with Meridian markings. "Found this in a lot I bought from a warehouse clearance. No idea what's on it. Haven't been able to crack the encryption. Two thousand credits and it's yours."

choices.text: Pay the 2,000 credits
Try to negotiate the price down
"That's too rich for me right now."

sq_011.text: You browse the shelves with deliberate nonchalance, examining old circuit boards and tangled cables as if you're just another scavenger looking for parts. Lucky watches you with professional disinterest. Near the back of the shop, behind a stack of defunct comm units, you notice a small locked display case. Inside, among other items, you can see the distinctive shape of a military-grade data chip. The price tag reads 2,500 credits, but the fact that it's locked away suggests Lucky knows it's worth more.

choices.text: Ask about the chip in the locked case
Try to pocket it while Lucky isn't looking

sq_012.text: You lean close to the cage wire and let your voice drop to something cold and final. Lucky's cybernetic eyes cycle through several zoom levels as he reassesses you. Then his hand moves under the counter and you hear the unmistakable whine of a weapon charging. "Friend, I've been robbed by professionals. You ain't that. But I tell you what — you want the chip, I'll sell it to you at a fair price. Two thousand. Take it or leave, but don't waste my time with threats."

choices.text: Pay the 2,000 credits
Back off and leave

sq_013.text: The Frequency is buried three levels deep in Neon Narrows, past the pleasure houses and the stim dens, through a door guarded by two augmented bouncers whose arms have been replaced with industrial-grade prosthetics. Bass vibrates through the walls. Inside, the club is a cathedral of sound and synthetic light. Bodies move on the dance floor like organisms in a petri dish. A bar runs the length of one wall, staffed by identical android bartenders. At the far end, a private booth is curtained off with smart-fabric that shifts between opaque and transparent.

choices.text: Approach the private booth directly
Order a drink and observe first
Ask a bartender about Silk

sq_014.text: The server farm stretches out before you in rows of dead machines, their carcasses picked clean by previous scavengers. But deeper in, past a collapsed section of ceiling, you find an intact server cluster still connected to a buried power line. A faint green light pulses on one of the racks. Someone has been here recently — fresh boot prints in the dust, a discarded ration wrapper. And there, slotted into a reader on the central server, is the first data chip, its Meridian military crest still visible under a film of grime.

choices.text: Take the chip carefully
Check for traps first

sq_015.text: You methodically check each rack as you move deeper into the server farm, pulling open access panels and scanning slots. Most are empty — stripped clean years ago. But your thoroughness reveals something else: fresh tool marks on several of the racks. Someone has been down here recently, and they were looking for the same thing you are. Footprints in the dust lead deeper into the facility, toward a section where emergency lighting still glows faintly.

choices.text: Follow the footprints
Proceed with extra caution

sq_016.text: The security drone rises from the shaft like a mechanical wasp, its targeting laser painting a red dot on your chest. It's an ancient model, corroded and stuttering, but the mounted stun cannon still looks functional. You dodge as it fires, the electrified bolt scorching the ground where you stood. One well-placed strike sends it spinning into a wall where it shatters into components. The threat is neutralized, but the noise might attract attention.

choices.text: Descend quickly into the server farm

sq_017.text: Credits transfer. Lucky slides the case under the cage wire with a grin that shows more chrome than tooth. "Pleasure doing business. You ever crack whatever's on that chip, you didn't get it from me, understood?" The chip feels heavier than it should as you pocket it. One down. The Collector will want to hear about this.

choices.text: Contact the Collector to report your find
Continue searching for the other chips

sq_018.text: "Twelve hundred," you counter, leaning against the cage with practiced ease. Lucky's eyes whir. "Eighteen." You shake your head. "Fourteen, and I won't mention to the sec-patrols that you're running unlicensed military hardware in a civilian shop." Lucky's smile disappears. He slides the case under the wire. "Fifteen hundred. Final. And you forget this address."

choices.text: Pay the 1,500 credits and take the chip

sq_019.text: Your fingers work with surgical precision, sliding the lock pick into the display case while Lucky argues with another customer about the price of a refurbished neural interface. The lock clicks open. You palm the chip and slip it into your pocket in one fluid motion. By the time Lucky turns back to you, you're browsing the shelves again with empty hands and an innocent expression.

choices.text: Leave the shop casually

sq_020.text: You push through the crowd toward the private booth. The smart-fabric curtain shimmers and goes transparent as you approach, revealing a slender figure reclining on synthetic leather. Silk is younger than you expected — early twenties, maybe, with hair shaved on one side and a neural jack port visible at the temple. Their eyes are entirely chrome, reflecting the club lights like liquid mercury. Two bodyguards flank the booth, arms folded.

choices.text: "I'm here about a data chip. Pre-Collapse military."
"Silk? I have a business proposition."

sq_021.text: You take a seat at the bar and order something cheap. The android bartender pours a fluorescent blue drink without expression. From here you can observe the private booth. Silk receives a steady stream of visitors — data traders, fixers, a few people who look desperate enough to be selling secrets. Each meeting lasts exactly three minutes. Silk's bodyguards are augmented heavies with subdermal armor plating. No easy approach.

choices.text: Wait for a gap between visitors and approach
Try to bribe one of the bodyguards

sq_022.text: The android bartender's face remains impassive, but their voice drops to a whisper. "Silk sees visitors by appointment. But they have a weakness — information. If you have something Silk doesn't know, you'll get an audience. Silk is currently interested in Meridian Corp's new security protocols for the Cloud Towers. Bring that, and the curtain opens."

choices.text: Approach the booth and bluff about having intel
Approach the booth directly

sq_023.text: You reach for the chip — and a voice cuts through the darkness behind you. "I'd leave that where it is if I were you." A figure steps from behind a server rack, weapon raised. A scavenger, by the look of them. Patched armor, wild eyes, a plasma cutter repurposed as a weapon. "I've been watching this chip for three weeks, waiting for the right buyer. You don't just walk in and take it."

choices.text: "Let's talk about this. We can split the profit."
Draw your weapon and face them down
Grab the chip and run

sq_024.text: Smart move. A hair-thin tripwire runs across the floor six inches in front of the server rack, connected to a crude fragmentation charge bolted to the underside of the rack above. Someone booby-trapped the chip. You carefully step over the wire and disable the charge, pocketing the detonator for good measure. The chip sits in its reader slot, pulsing with a faint green light, undisturbed for years.

choices.text: Extract the chip carefully

sq_025.text: You ping the frequency on the Collector's card. The response is immediate: coordinates for a dead drop in an abandoned transit station. You leave the chip in a sealed container at the designated spot. Twenty minutes later, your account receives a credit transfer — five thousand credits, as promised. A text message follows: "Excellent work. The others await. Do not disappoint me."

choices.text: Search for the next chip
Take a break from the Collector's errands

sq_026.text: Silk's chrome eyes fix on you with an intensity that borders on predatory. "Ah. The Collector sent you." It's not a question. "That chip is worth far more than whatever that masked corpse is paying you. It contains the encryption keys for Meridian's original stack network — the one they built before the Collapse. In the right hands, those keys could unlock every memory ever stored in the old system. Millions of dead minds, accessible again."

choices.text: "Name your price for the chip."
"What if I took it by force?"
"What would you want in trade?"

sq_027.text: Your confidence gets you past the bodyguards. Silk gestures to the seat opposite with a languid wave. "You carry yourself like someone with something to sell. I'm listening. But understand — I charge for my time. If what you're offering isn't worth it, this conversation gets expensive for you." Those chrome eyes scan you like a document reader, searching for tells.

choices.text: "I'm here about a specific data chip."
Offer fabricated intel about Meridian Corp

sq_028.text: You sidle up to the smaller of Silk's two bodyguards and mention a number. The guard's expression doesn't change, but they lean slightly toward you. "Silk's in a good mood tonight. I'll let you in, but you cause trouble, I'll break you in ways a doctor can't fix. Understood?" They step aside just enough for you to slip through to the booth.

choices.text: Approach Silk

sq_029.text: The scavenger's weapon wavers. Greed wars with caution behind those wild eyes. "Split? You're offering to split?" They lower the plasma cutter an inch. "The Collector's paying five thousand per chip. You give me half, and you can walk out of here with it. Twenty-five hundred credits. Transfer now, and we both leave breathing."

choices.text: Pay 2,500 credits and take the chip
"How about I pay you when I get paid?"

sq_030.text: The scavenger snarls and lunges. But they're desperate, not trained. You sidestep the wild swing of the plasma cutter and deliver a sharp strike that sends them sprawling against a server rack. The rack topples, crashing into the next one in a domino cascade that fills the room with dust and sparks. The scavenger doesn't get up. They're breathing, but they won't be a problem. The chip is yours.

choices.text: Take the chip and leave

sq_031.text: You snatch the chip from the reader and bolt. The scavenger screams something obscene and fires the plasma cutter. A bolt of superheated gas scorches the wall where your head was a millisecond ago. You vault a fallen server rack, slide under a collapsed beam, and sprint for the ventilation shaft. Behind you, heavy footsteps and labored breathing. But you're faster. You explode out of the shaft into gray daylight, the chip clutched in your fist.

choices.text: Contact the Collector
Pocket the chip and move on

sq_032.text: The chip slots into your palm, warm from the server's residual heat. It's smaller than you expected — barely the size of a thumbnail — but the weight of what it might contain makes it feel like a stone. You pocket it carefully. One chip down. The Collector will be waiting.

choices.text: Contact the Collector
Hold onto the chip for now

sq_033.text: Silk laughs — a sound like breaking crystal. "Credits? You think I want credits? The chip stays with me. But I'll tell you what — bring me something I can't get elsewhere, and we'll negotiate. I want the access codes to Meridian's Sector 9 surveillance grid. Get me those, and the chip is yours. Otherwise..." Silk makes a dismissive gesture. "This conversation is over."

choices.text: "I'll get your codes."
"There has to be another way."
Leave the Frequency empty-handed

sq_034.text: Silk doesn't flinch. The bodyguards do — both stepping forward with weapons drawn faster than you can track. "Violence? In my club?" Silk smiles, showing perfect teeth. "I have forty-seven armed employees in this building. The bouncers at the door are former military augments. The bartender androids are loaded with paralytic aerosol. You wouldn't make it to the street." The smile vanishes. "Sit down. Or don't. But drop the posturing."

choices.text: Sit down and negotiate properly
Leave before things get worse

sq_035.text: Silk leans forward, chrome eyes gleaming. "A trade, then. I appreciate pragmatism. Here's what I need: Meridian Corp runs a surveillance grid in Sector 9. I need the access codes to that grid. I have... interests in that sector that require privacy. Bring me those codes, and the chip is yours. No credits change hands. A clean exchange."

choices.text: "Deal. How do I get the codes?"
"That's corporate security. I'd need an army."

sq_036.text: You weave a convincing tale of intercepted Meridian communications — security rotations, patrol frequencies, executive travel schedules. All fabricated, but delivered with enough technical detail to pass surface scrutiny. Silk's chrome eyes narrow with interest. "Useful. If it checks out. I'll have my people verify. In the meantime..." Silk reaches into a coat pocket and produces a data chip. "A gesture of good faith. Take it. But if your intel is fabricated, I will find you."

choices.text: Take the chip and leave quickly
"It's solid intel. Pleasure doing business."

sq_037.text: Silk provides coordinates for a Meridian relay station in Sector 9's industrial zone. "The codes rotate every six hours. You'll need to jack into the relay directly and pull them during a rotation window. The next window opens at midnight. Security is a skeleton crew — four guards, two drones. Manageable, if you're competent." Silk waves a hand. "Midnight. Don't be late."

choices.text: Head to the relay station
Prepare first — gather supplies

sq_038.text: "An army? No. A brain." Silk taps their temple. "The relay station in Sector 9's industrial zone has a skeleton crew at midnight during code rotation. Four guards, two drones. A single competent operative could manage it. I'm offering you the chip the Collector wants in exchange for an hour's work. That's the best deal you'll get in this city."

choices.text: "Fine. I'll do it."
"I need to think about this."

sq_039.text: You pocket the chip and exit the Frequency without looking back. The bass fades behind you as you climb back toward the surface streets. Three chips now — or close to it. The Collector will pay handsomely. But Silk's warning echoes in your mind. When the fabricated intel fails to check out, and it will, Silk will come looking. You've bought yourself time. The question is how much.

choices.text: Contact the Collector with all the chips
Lay low for a while

sq_040.text: The Meridian relay station is a squat concrete bunker bristling with antennae. True to Silk's intel, the midnight crew is minimal: four bored guards playing cards in the security booth, and two patrol drones making lazy circles around the perimeter. A maintenance hatch on the roof offers potential access to the relay's internal systems.

choices.text: Sneak past the guards to the maintenance hatch
Create a distraction to draw the guards away
Fight through the front entrance

sq_041.text: You move like smoke through the shadows, timing your approach between drone sweeps. The maintenance hatch opens with a whisper, and you drop into a service corridor lined with blinking relay equipment. The access terminal is at the far end, its screen casting blue light across the narrow space. Your fingers fly across the keys, pulling the surveillance codes as they cycle through rotation. Download complete.

choices.text: Return the codes to Silk

sq_042.text: You rig a discarded fuel cell from a nearby dumpster to blow in thirty seconds, toss it across the perimeter fence, and flatten yourself against the bunker wall. The explosion lights up the night. Guards pour out of the security booth, weapons drawn, running toward the blast site. The patrol drones pivot and streak toward the disturbance. You slip through the unguarded door and find the relay terminal. Codes downloaded in under a minute.

choices.text: Return the codes to Silk

sq_043.text: Subtlety was never your strong suit. You crash through the front entrance and put down two guards before the other two can draw. The drones converge, but in the confined space of the bunker, their maneuverability is limited. You smash one with a fire extinguisher and shoot the other. The relay terminal is right there. Codes downloaded. Alarms blaring. Time to go.

choices.text: Return the codes to Silk

sq_044.text: Silk examines the codes on a portable terminal, chrome eyes flickering with reflected data streams. "Authentic. Complete. You've exceeded my expectations." The data chip slides across the table toward you. "Our business is concluded. You may tell the Collector that Silk sends regards." A thin smile. "And if you ever need work again... you know where to find me."

choices.text: Take the chip and contact the Collector
Take the chip and leave

sq_045.text: Your comm device buzzes with a message from an unknown sender. The text is fragmented, corrupted, barely readable: "HELP ME... I'M STILL HERE... THEY THINK I'M DEAD BUT I'M TRAPPED IN THE SIGNAL... PLEASE... SECTOR 4 RELAY TOWER... FIND MY BODY..." The sender ID resolves to a name: Kael Voss. A quick search confirms it — Kael Voss, age 34, pronounced dead six months ago after a stack transfer malfunction. Officially, a closed case.

choices.text: Investigate Sector 4 relay tower
Research Kael Voss's death records
Delete the message and move on

sq_046.text: The Sector 4 relay tower is a skeletal structure of steel and cable, rising from a rooftop in the warehouse district. At its base, a maintenance terminal flickers with static. As you approach, your comm device erupts with garbled audio — fragments of a voice, desperate and distorted. The terminal screen displays scrolling text: coordinates, timestamps, and a repeating phrase: STACK FRAGMENT DETECTED — QUARANTINE PROTOCOL ACTIVE.

choices.text: Access the quarantined stack fragment
Search the area around the tower base

sq_047.text: The public records paint a clinical picture. Kael Voss, systems engineer for Meridian Corp's stack infrastructure division. Died during a routine memory transfer when the receiving stack suffered a cascade failure. The official cause: complete neural dissolution. No recoverable engram data. Body cremated per corporate protocol. But the coroner's report has irregularities — the neural scan was signed off by a Dr. Elara Shin, who resigned from Meridian two weeks later.

choices.text: Try to find Dr. Elara Shin
Head to the Sector 4 relay tower

sq_048.text: You bypass the quarantine protocols and make contact with the fragment. The terminal's speakers crackle, and then a voice comes through — thin, wavering, but unmistakably human. "Oh god, you're real. You're actually real. I've been sending that message for months. My name is Kael Voss and I am not dead. My consciousness was shattered during the transfer, but a fragment survived, trapped in the relay network. I'm... I'm like a ghost in the wires. Please, you have to help me. I need a body. Or a stack. Something to transfer into."

choices.text: "What happened to your original body?"
"How do I know you're really Kael Voss?"
"I'll help you. What do you need?"

sq_049.text: At the base of the tower, half-hidden by debris, you find a personal effects bag with Meridian Corp markings. Inside: a cracked data pad, a worn photograph of a woman and a young boy, and a small neural interface adapter that still carries a faint charge. The data pad boots up to reveal a final diary entry from Kael Voss, written the morning of the fatal transfer: "Something about this transfer feels wrong. Dr. Shin was acting strange. If anything happens to me, tell Mara I love her. Tell my son I'm sorry."

choices.text: Try to contact the fragment through the adapter
Look for Mara Voss

sq_050.text: Dr. Elara Shin isn't easy to find. After leaving Meridian, she dropped off the grid entirely. But a few inquiries at the right clinics in the Lower District turn up a lead: a disgraced neurosurgeon running a back-alley clinic in Sector 7, treating stack sickness patients for cheap. You find her in a cramped office that smells of antiseptic and regret. She's older than her Meridian ID photo, eyes hollow, hands shaking slightly. She sees you and her face goes white. "You've been getting the messages too, haven't you?"

choices.text: "What do you know about Kael Voss's death?"
"What messages? Tell me everything."

sq_051.text: The voice in the relay crackles with static-laden anguish. "Cremated. Meridian cremated it within hours of the 'accident.' Standard protocol, they said. But it wasn't an accident. Dr. Shin was supposed to transfer me to a new stack — a prototype. Instead, the transfer fragmented my consciousness and scattered it across the relay network. I think... I think Meridian wanted to study what happens when a consciousness breaks apart. I was an experiment."

choices.text: "Can you be reassembled?"
"I'll find Dr. Shin and get answers."

sq_052.text: "Ask me anything. My wife's name is Mara. My son is Ren, he's seven now — no, eight. God, has it been that long? I worked on the Series-7 stack architecture. My employee ID was MER-7734-VS. I had a scar on my left hand from when I caught it in a server rack during my first week." The voice breaks. "I know what I sound like. A corrupted engram, a ghost in the machine. But I remember everything. I remember the fear when the transfer went wrong. Please."

choices.text: "I believe you. How can I help?"
"I need to verify this with your family."

sq_053.text: "There's a blank stack — a clean one, no previous owner — in Meridian's Sector 4 warehouse. Confiscated goods, scheduled for destruction. If you can get me that stack and bring it back to this relay, I can attempt to reassemble myself into it. It's risky. Fragments of me are scattered across six relay nodes. But if you bridge them through this terminal while the blank stack is connected..." A pause filled with digital noise. "It might work. It has to work."

choices.text: "I'll get the blank stack."
"This sounds dangerous. What are the risks?"

sq_054.text: Mara Voss lives in a cramped apartment in Sector 5 with her eight-year-old son Ren. When you mention Kael's name, her face cycles through shock, grief, anger, and finally a desperate, trembling hope. "Messages? Kael's been... sending messages?" She grabs your arm. "His consciousness survived? That's — that's not possible. They told me his engram was destroyed. They told me —" She breaks down. Ren watches from the doorway with large, frightened eyes.

choices.text: "I believe he's alive, in a way. I want to help."
"I'm sorry. Maybe it's better to let him go."

sq_055.text: Dr. Shin pours herself a drink with trembling hands. "It wasn't supposed to happen. The prototype stack had a defect — I flagged it, I told them, but the project lead pushed the timeline. Kael's consciousness fragmented during transfer. Instead of dying cleanly, he... shattered. Pieces of him scattered across the relay network like shrapnel." She looks at you with haunted eyes. "I've been receiving his messages ever since. Fragments. Screams. Pleas for help. That's why I left Meridian. I couldn't — I can't —"

choices.text: "Can you help reassemble him?"
"Is there a way to end his suffering?"

sq_056.text: "Kael Voss was my patient. My responsibility. The transfer that killed him — that fragmented him — used a prototype stack I knew was defective. I reported the defect. Corporate overruled me. And now his consciousness is scattered across the relay network in pieces, sending distress signals to anyone who'll listen." She drains her glass. "I've spent six months trying to figure out how to reassemble him. I think I've found a way. But I need help."

choices.text: "I'm in. What's the plan?"
"Why should I trust you?"

sq_057.text: Meridian's Sector 4 warehouse is a fortress of corrugated steel and razor wire. But the loading dock has a blind spot where two camera angles fail to overlap — you exploit it, slipping inside while the automated security drones pass on their programmed routes. The warehouse is stacked floor to ceiling with confiscated tech. In aisle 47, rack 12, you find them: blank memory stacks, still sealed in their anti-static packaging. You pocket one and ghost back out the way you came.

choices.text: Return to the relay tower with the blank stack

sq_058.text: "The risks?" A bitter laugh dissolves into static. "I'm a fragmented consciousness trapped in a relay network. Every day I lose coherence. Every day there's a little less of me. In six months, maybe a year, there won't be enough left to reassemble. The risk of doing this is that the transfer fails and what's left of me dissipates entirely. The risk of not doing this is that I fade away piece by piece until I'm nothing but noise in the signal. I'd rather take my chances."

choices.text: "I'll get the blank stack. Hang on."
"I need to think about this."

sq_059.text: Dr. Shin nods slowly. "There's a way to terminate the fragments. A kill signal broadcast through the relay network. It would be... painless. Like turning off a light. Kael would simply stop existing." She opens a drawer and produces a small transmitter. "I built this months ago. I just couldn't bring myself to use it. If you think that's the right choice..." Her hand extends, offering the device. A mercy, or a murder. The line between them has never been thinner.

choices.text: Take the device and end Kael's suffering
"No. We try to save him first."

sq_060.text: Dr. Shin's plan is precise: a blank memory stack connected to the Sector 4 relay terminal, with her custom bridging software to pull the scattered fragments together. "I've mapped six relay nodes that contain fragments of Kael's consciousness. The software will ping each one simultaneously, drawing the fragments through the network into the blank stack. It should take about ninety seconds. During that time, the terminal will be vulnerable — if anyone interrupts the process, the fragments will scatter permanently."

choices.text: "I'll get the blank stack and meet you there."
"What if it doesn't work?"

sq_061.text: You connect the blank stack to the relay terminal. The screen floods with data. Kael's voice comes through, stronger now, almost coherent: "I can feel it. The other fragments. They're out there, waiting. Do it. Start the transfer." You initiate the bridging sequence. The terminal screams with activity — data pouring in from six relay nodes simultaneously. The blank stack's indicator light flickers from red to amber. Ninety seconds feels like ninety years. Then — green. The transfer is complete.

choices.text: Check if Kael is intact

sq_062.text: You climb the relay tower one final time and plug the transmitter into the maintenance terminal. The kill signal broadcasts across the network — a single pulse that reaches every node where Kael's fragments reside. For a moment, the comm device in your pocket fills with sound: a cascade of memories, emotions, half-formed thoughts, all rushing together and then falling silent. The terminal screen displays: FRAGMENTS TERMINATED — SIGNAL CLEAR. Kael Voss is finally, truly dead.

choices.text: Leave the tower in silence

sq_063.text: The stack hums in your hand. From its tiny speaker comes a voice — clear, whole, unmistakably alive: "I'm... I'm here. All of me. I can remember everything — Mara's face, Ren's laugh, the way the sun looked from the towers. God." A long, shaking breath that shouldn't be possible from a stack without a body. "Thank you. I need a body — a synth chassis, something I can interface with. But that can wait. For now... for now I'm whole. That's enough."

choices.text: Bring the stack to Mara Voss
Keep the stack and move on

sq_064.text: Mara holds the stack in both hands, tears streaming down her face. Ren presses his ear against it, listening to his father's voice with wide, wondering eyes. "Daddy? You sound funny." Kael laughs — a sound that fills the small apartment like sunlight. "I know, buddy. I sound like a really expensive radio. But I'm here. I'm not going anywhere." Mara looks at you over her son's head, and the gratitude in her eyes is worth more than any credits you've ever earned.

choices.text: Leave the family to their reunion

sq_065.text: The message comes through a dead-drop at a noodle stand in the Lower District. "Rooftop, Building 7-C, Sector 3. Come alone. Bring water." The coordinates lead you to a crumbling residential tower. Fifteen flights of stairs past graffiti and sleeping vagrants bring you to a rusted door that opens onto the roof. And there it is — the Last Garden. Rows of green, impossibly vibrant against the gray cityscape. Tomatoes, herbs, flowers you've never seen outside of old photographs. A woman in dirt-stained coveralls stands among them, watching you with wary eyes.

choices.text: "This is... incredible. How?"
"You asked for water. I brought some."

sq_066.text: The woman — she introduces herself as Yara — gestures proudly at the rows of living green. "Thirty years of work. My mother started it. Smuggled seeds from the botanical archives before Meridian sealed them. The soil is synthetic, mixed from composted waste and mineral supplements I cook in the lab downstairs. The water is collected rainwater, filtered through carbon and ceramic." Her face darkens. "But Meridian's found out. They've declared all unauthorized agriculture a biosecurity threat. They're sending a demolition crew. Tomorrow."

choices.text: "We have to stop them. What's the plan?"
"Can you relocate the garden?"
"I'm sorry, but I can't fight Meridian Corp."

sq_067.text: Yara takes the water containers with visible relief. "Thank you. The filtration system broke last week, and I've been rationing what's left. These plants are the last living flora in the Lower District. Maybe in the whole city, outside the corp gardens in the towers." She pours water carefully along a row of tomato plants, each drop measured and precious. "But water isn't the real problem anymore. Meridian's coming. They want to destroy all of this. I need help."

choices.text: "Tell me everything."
"What can one person do against a corp?"

sq_068.text: Yara pulls up a holographic schematic of the building on a battered tablet. "The demolition order was signed by a mid-level Meridian bureaucrat named Director Hask. He doesn't care about plants — he wants the rooftop real estate for a signal relay installation. If we can make the demolition more trouble than it's worth, or find leverage against Hask, the garden survives." She outlines three options: blockade the building entrance, forge a counter-order, or find dirt on Hask.

choices.text: "Let's blockade. Rally the residents."
"I can forge a counter-order."
"Finding dirt on a corp director. That's my specialty."

sq_069.text: Yara shakes her head. "Thirty years of ecosystem balance. The soil bacteria, the root networks, the pollination cycles I've carefully maintained — you can't just uproot it. Moving the garden would kill half the plants outright. This rooftop has the only unobstructed sunlight angle in three blocks. It's this location or nothing." She kneels beside a flowering plant and touches its leaves gently. "These are the last of their kind. If they die here, they die forever."

choices.text: "Then we defend it. What's the plan?"
"Maybe save what seeds you can."

sq_070.text: You spend the night going door to door in the building, talking to residents. The garden has been a quiet secret for decades — many tenants have eaten Yara's tomatoes, used her herbs. By morning, forty-seven people are gathered in the lobby, arms linked, blocking the entrance. When the demolition crew arrives — four workers and two Meridian security guards — they find a wall of defiant faces. The crew leader radios for instructions. An hour passes. Then two. Finally, the crew leaves. Director Hask has decided this isn't worth a public incident. For now.

choices.text: Return to Yara with the good news

sq_071.text: Using a stolen Meridian letterhead template and a forged authorization code, you create a counter-order reclassifying the rooftop as a "protected heritage site" under a regulation so obscure that no one in the bureaucracy will question it for months. The document is uploaded to Meridian's internal system through a vulnerability you exploit in their document management portal. When the demolition crew arrives, they scan the order, exchange confused glances, and leave. Red tape has never been more beautiful.

choices.text: Return to Yara with the good news

sq_072.text: Director Hask is a creature of habit and vice. A few hours of surveillance and a well-placed bribe to his secretary reveal that Hask has been embezzling from Meridian's infrastructure fund — skimming credits from relay installations and funneling them through shell companies. You compile the evidence onto a data pad and arrange a meeting at a public café. When you slide the pad across the table, Hask's face goes the color of old concrete. "The garden stays," you say. "Or this goes to your superiors." He nods, once, and the demolition order is quietly rescinded.

choices.text: Return to Yara with the good news

sq_073.text: Yara's eyes fill with tears, but she nods with grim practicality. "You're right. If the garden falls, the seeds survive." Together, you spend the night harvesting seeds from every plant, sealing them in airtight containers with preservation gel. Yara packages over two hundred seed varieties — a genetic library of pre-Collapse flora. "Find somewhere safe for these. A vault, a bunker, somewhere Meridian can't reach. If someone, someday, rebuilds... these seeds are the beginning."

choices.text: Take the seeds and protect them

sq_074.text: Yara is on her knees in the soil, hands black with dirt, a look of fierce joy on her face. "They left? They actually left?" She stands and pulls you into a hug that smells of earth and green things. "You've saved something precious today. Not just plants — the idea that living things still matter in this city of chrome and concrete." She presses a bundle of fresh tomatoes into your hands. "Come back anytime. There will always be something growing here for you."

choices.text: Leave the garden with a rare sense of hope

sq_075.text: A flyer materializes in your hand, slipped there by a passing stranger who vanishes into the crowd before you can react. The paper is old-fashioned — actual printed ink on synthetic fiber. THE PIT — EVERY FRIDAY — SUBLEVEL 9, SECTOR 6 — FIGHT FOR GLORY, FIGHT FOR CREDITS — ALL CHALLENGERS WELCOME. Below the text, a crude illustration shows two figures locked in combat inside a cage. The address leads to one of the deepest sublevels in the city, where even sec-drones rarely venture.

choices.text: Go to the Pit and check it out
Underground fighting rings aren't your thing

sq_076.text: Sublevel 9 is hot, loud, and reeks of sweat and machine oil. The Pit itself is a converted cargo bay, its floor stained dark with old blood. A cage of reinforced steel stands at the center, illuminated by harsh industrial lights. Hundreds of spectators crowd the surrounding platforms, screaming and waving credit chips. A massive orc in a referee's vest spots you and grins. "Fresh meat! You here to watch or to bleed? Fighters get five hundred credits per win. Three wins and you fight the champion. Beat the champ, you get ten thousand."

choices.text: "I'm here to fight."
"I'll watch first."
"Who's the champion?"

sq_077.text: The orc referee slaps your back hard enough to rattle your teeth. "That's the spirit! First bout's in ten minutes. Your opponent is a dock worker named Gritz — hits hard, slow on the feet. Rules are simple: no lethal weapons, no lethal augments, fight until one of you can't stand. Everything else is fair game." He shoves you toward a changing area that's really just a curtain hung between two shipping containers. Beyond it, the crowd roars as the previous fight ends with a sickening crunch.

choices.text: Step into the cage and fight Gritz
Use speed and technique instead of brute force

sq_078.text: You find a spot on the upper platform with a clear view of the cage. The current fight is between a wiry woman with cybernetic legs and a hulking man covered in ritual scars. The woman is faster, dancing around her opponent's haymakers and landing precise strikes to his joints. Three minutes in, the big man drops to one knee. The crowd screams for blood, but the woman steps back and lets the referee call it. Skill over savagery. You note her technique — it might be useful.

choices.text: Sign up for a fight
Leave the Pit

sq_079.text: The referee's grin widens. "The champ? That's Raze. Undefeated in forty-seven fights. Former Meridian security operative, dishonorably discharged for excessive force. Let that sink in — a corp goon thrown out for being too violent. Subdermal armor, reinforced skeleton, pain inhibitors. Fighting Raze is like fighting a wall that hits back." He leans closer. "But between us, Raze has a weak spot — the left knee. Old injury. Never got it fixed. You didn't hear that from me."

choices.text: "Sign me up. I'll work my way to Raze."
"I'll pass on fighting a murder machine."

sq_080.text: Gritz is big, angry, and predictable. The dock worker charges like a freight hauler, arms swinging. You weather the storm — taking hits that shake your vision, giving back calculated strikes that wear down his guard. The crowd screams with every impact. Blood and sweat fly. After four brutal minutes, Gritz drops to all fours, gasping. The referee counts. It's over. You stand in the cage, chest heaving, the roar of the crowd washing over you like a wave. First fight: won.

choices.text: Fight the second opponent
Take your credits and leave

sq_081.text: Your second opponent is a synth called Needle — lean, fast, with retractable blades hidden in their forearms. They fight with terrifying precision, each strike aimed at tendons and pressure points. The crowd goes quiet, sensing something different. This isn't a brawl — it's surgery. You take hits that send electric pain down your limbs. But you land hits too, targeting the synth's exposed joints where the chassis plating is thinnest. Needle staggers. You press the advantage. The synth drops. Second fight: won.

choices.text: Face the champion — Raze
You're hurt. Take the credits and walk away.

sq_082.text: Raze enters the cage to thunderous applause. The champion is a wall of muscle and metal, eyes dead as spent cartridges. No taunts, no posturing — just a cold, mechanical assessment. The fight begins. Raze is everything they warned you about: fast for their size, brutally strong, seemingly impervious to pain. You eat punches that feel like being hit by a transport. But you keep moving, keep striking. You target the left knee, and something gives — Raze stumbles, just for a second. You exploit the opening with everything you have left. Raze goes down. The Pit erupts.

choices.text: Collect your winnings — ten thousand credits

sq_083.text: The referee counts out your credits with a disappointed shake of his head. "Could've been a contender. Come back when you've got more fight in you." The credits feel good in your account, but the bruises and the taste of blood remind you of the cost. The Pit's crowd has already forgotten you, turning its attention to the next bout. In the Lower District, entertainment is cheap and fighters are disposable.

choices.text: Leave the Pit behind

sq_084.text: Ten thousand credits hit your account as the crowd chants your name — or the name they've given you, some guttural syllable that sounds like violence itself. The former champion Raze sits against the cage wall, nursing a shattered knee, watching you with something that might be respect. The referee throws an arm around your shoulders. "New champion of the Pit! You want the title, you defend it every Friday. Or you walk away rich. Your choice." The crowd awaits your answer.

choices.text: "I'll defend the title."
"I'll take the credits. I'm done."

sq_085.text: The crowd erupts. You're the new champion of the Pit — a title that carries weight in the underworld, where reputation is currency. Fighters will come from across the districts to challenge you. The credits will flow. But so will the blood, and the line between sport and savagery gets thinner with every fight. You've stepped into a world that chews people up and spits out what's left. But for now, standing in the cage with ten thousand credits and the roar of the crowd, it feels like victory.

choices.text: Leave the Pit as champion

sq_086.text: The woman sits in the darkest corner of a tea house in Sector 2, her face obscured by a hooded jacket. She looks up as you approach, and you catch a glimpse of eyes red-rimmed from crying. "Please," she says before you can speak. "My daughter. Lira. She's twelve. She went to the market in the Lower District three days ago and never came home. The sec-corps don't care — she's nobody to them. Just another missing kid from the slums. But she's everything to me. Please."

choices.text: "Tell me everything about Lira."
"Where was she last seen?"
"I wish I could help, but..."

sq_087.text: The mother — her name is Dani — shows you a holographic photo. Lira is small for twelve, with dark braided hair and a gap-toothed smile. She was wearing a red jacket and carrying a canvas bag. She left home at midday to buy protein paste at the Sector 7 market. The route takes her through three blocks of Lower District streets. "She's smart," Dani insists, voice cracking. "She knows to avoid the gang corridors. She knows the safe routes. Something happened. Someone took her."

choices.text: Head to the Sector 7 market to investigate
Ask around the neighborhood first

sq_088.text: The Sector 7 market is a chaotic sprawl of vendor stalls and shouting merchants. You show Lira's photo to every vendor on the block. Most shrug or shake their heads. But an old woman selling recycled fabric remembers: "Red jacket? Yes. She bought paste from Goro's stall, then headed south toward the transit corridor. But I saw a van — unmarked, black — pull up near the alley entrance. Two people got out. Big people. They went the same direction as the girl." She points south. The transit corridor leads to the lower sublevels.

choices.text: Follow the trail south toward the sublevels
Search for security camera footage of the van

sq_089.text: A teenager in the building's stairwell remembers seeing Lira three days ago. "She was running. Scared. Two adults chasing her — they had those body-snatcher vans, you know? The ones the organ harvesters use." The teen's eyes are wide and knowing. In the Lower District, organ harvesting rings are an open secret — kidnapping street kids and stripping them for parts to sell on the black market. The teen gives you a location: a warehouse in Sublevel 4 where the vans have been seen coming and going.

choices.text: Head directly to the Sublevel 4 warehouse
Gather backup first

sq_090.text: The transit corridor descends into the sublevels like a throat swallowing you into the city's guts. The lighting gets worse with every level — from flickering fluorescent to nothing but the glow of your comm device. On Sublevel 3, you find Lira's canvas bag, discarded in a corridor junction. Empty. A few meters away, scuff marks on the floor and a smear of something dark on the wall. Someone was dragged. The trail leads to a service elevator that only goes one direction: down to Sublevel 4.

choices.text: Take the elevator down
Search for another way into Sublevel 4

sq_091.text: You hack into the nearest traffic management node and pull camera footage from the day Lira disappeared. The van is there — black, unmarked, with scrambled plates. But the camera catches a partial logo on the side door: a caduceus — the medical symbol — with a red X through it. You've seen that symbol before, tagged on walls in the sublevels. It's the mark of the Red Harvest, one of the most feared organ-harvesting rings in the Lower District. They operate from a warehouse on Sublevel 4.

choices.text: Head to Sublevel 4 immediately

sq_092.text: The Sublevel 4 warehouse is guarded by two thugs with stun batons and augmented reflexes. Inside, through a grimy window, you can see a makeshift surgical theater — operating tables, medical equipment, and a row of cryo-pods where kidnap victims are kept sedated until they're harvested. In one of the pods, small and still, you can see a red jacket. Lira is alive. But the clock is ticking — a surgical team is prepping at one of the tables.

choices.text: Fight through the guards
Sneak in through the ventilation system
Create a diversion to draw them out

sq_093.text: A maintenance shaft runs parallel to the elevator, descending through the levels in a maze of pipes and conduits. You navigate it by feel in near-total darkness, emerging through a floor grate directly inside the Sublevel 4 warehouse. You're behind a stack of crates, with a clear view of the operation: surgical tables, cryo-pods, and a girl in a red jacket sleeping in stasis. The harvesters haven't noticed you yet.

choices.text: Free Lira from the cryo-pod
Take out the harvesters first

sq_094.text: Violence erupts in the sterile space like an infection. The guards are tough, augmented, but you're fighting for a child's life and that makes you faster, harder, meaner. Stun batons crackle and miss. Your fists don't. The surgical team scatters, screaming. In ninety seconds, the warehouse is yours. You wrench open Lira's cryo-pod and she tumbles out, groggy but alive, blinking up at you with confused, frightened eyes. "I want my mom," she whispers.

choices.text: Carry Lira to safety and contact her mother

sq_095.text: You ghost through the warehouse like a shadow, moving between cover until you reach the cryo-pods. The release mechanism is simple — a manual override beside each pod. You pull it, and the pod hisses open. Lira's eyes flutter, unfocused from the sedative. You lift her, small and light, and carry her toward the exit before the harvesters notice the empty pod. By the time someone shouts an alarm, you're already in the elevator, doors closing, ascending toward safety.

choices.text: Get Lira back to her mother

sq_096.text: You overload a power junction in the corridor outside, killing the lights in the warehouse. In the confusion, you slip past the guards as they stumble in the darkness, reach the cryo-pods by memory and touch, and release Lira. She's barely conscious, mumbling through the sedative haze. You guide her through the dark toward the emergency exit, navigating by the faint glow of your comm device. Behind you, the harvesters are still shouting in the darkness. You're gone before the backup generator kicks in.

choices.text: Get Lira back to her mother

sq_097.text: Dani opens the door and collapses to her knees. Lira, still wobbly from the sedative, falls into her mother's arms. They hold each other on the floor of the apartment, crying, and you stand in the doorway feeling something unfamiliar in this city of chrome and cruelty — something warm. Dani looks up at you through her tears. "I don't have much, but whatever I have is yours. You brought her back. You brought my baby back."

choices.text: "No payment needed. Keep your daughter safe."
Accept Dani's gratitude and leave quietly

sq_098.text: A voice calls your name from an alley you thought was empty. You turn, hand on your weapon. A figure steps forward — older, weathered, with a face that tugs at something buried deep in your memory. "It's been a long time," they say. "You probably don't remember me. But I remember you. And you owe me. I helped you once, years ago, when nobody else would. Now I need the same." Their eyes are desperate. "Meridian's after me. I need to disappear. Tonight."

choices.text: "Who are you? I don't remember."
"What do you need from me?"
"I can't help you. I'm sorry."

sq_099.text: "My name is Corvin. I ran a safehouse in Sector 9 back when you were nobody — just another kid trying to survive the Lower District. I fed you, kept you off the streets for three months. You were too young, maybe, too desperate to remember." Corvin's voice cracks. "But I remember. And now I need somewhere to hide. Meridian's found evidence of my resistance connections. They'll come for me before dawn. I need passage out of the city or a place deep enough that they can't find me."

choices.text: "I'll help you get out of the city."
"I know a place you can hide."

sq_100.text: You lead Corvin through the back alleys and maintenance tunnels you know by heart, past patrol drones and sec-checkpoints, into the deep sublevels where Meridian's reach grows thin. In a forgotten utility chamber, behind three locked doors and a collapsed stairwell, you find a space that's almost safe. Corvin grips your hand with both of theirs. "I knew you'd come through. Some debts, the ones that matter, people always pay." They disappear into the darkness, and you're left standing alone with the weight of a past you barely remember and a debt you didn't know you owed. Paid in full.

choices.text: Return to the surface
